concepts.title: Solution of a Differential Equation(General Solution and Particular Solution)
concepts.conceptBody:
Solution of a Differential Equation

Any relation between dependent and independent variables which when substituted in the differential equation, reduce it to an identity is called a solution of the differential equation. A solution of a differential equation does not contain the derivatives of the dependent variables with respect to the independent variable.

Example : 

Consider the differential equation

$\frac{{{d}^{2}}y}{d{{x}^{2}}}+y=0$ ...(i)

Let $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{A} \sin \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{B} \cos \mathrm{x}$, where $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ are arbitrary constant ...(ii)

Differentiating (ii) twice w.r.t. x, we get

$\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}=-A\sin \text{x}-\text{B}\cos \text{x }$

$\Rightarrow $ $\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}-\text{y }\Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{\text{d}}^{2}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}+\text{y}=0$

Therefore $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{A} \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{B} \cos \mathrm{x}$ is a solution of differential equation (i).

It can be easily seen that $y=3 \sin x-2 \cos x, y=B \cos x$ are all solutions of (i). The most general of these solution is (ii), all others are particular solutions of the differential equation (i).

concepts.title: Method of Solving First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation when dy/dx=f(x)
concepts.conceptBody:
Types of Differential Equations

Type-1 Differential Equation of type $\frac{dy}{dx}=f(x)$

This is a very simple type which can be solved easily by using

$\int{d}y=\int{f}(x)dx+C$

concepts.title: Method of Solving First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation when dy/dx=f(y)
concepts.conceptBody:
Method of Solving First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation when dy/dx=f(y) 

Type-2 Differential equation of the type $\frac{dy}{dx}=f(y)$

This is the same type as above with the only change in functions on R.H.S. Here we solve it by the following manner.

If, $\frac{dy}{dx}=f(y)$ then

$dx=\frac{dy}{f(y)}\Rightarrow \int{d}x=\int{\frac{dy}{f(y)}}+C$

concepts.title: Method of Solving First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation when it is variable separable
concepts.conceptBody:
Method of Solving First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation when it is variable separable

Type-3 Equation in variable separable form $\frac{dy}{dx}=f(x)$

In this type the differential equation exists as product of two separate functions of $\text{x }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ y}$ respectively in R.H.S. We solve them as follow,

$\frac{dy}{dx}=f(x)g(y)$

⇒ $\frac{dy}{g(y)}=f(x)dx$

⇒ $\int{\frac{dy}{g(y)}}=\int{f}(x)dx+C$

Other forms of this could be

$\int{g}(y)dy=\int{f}(x)dx+C\text{ }$

or $\int{g}(y)dy=\int{\frac{dx}{f(x)}}+C$

concepts.title: First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation reducible to variable separable form
concepts.conceptBody:
First Order First Degree Linear Differential Equation reducible to variable separable form

Type-4 Equation reducible to variable separable form i.e . $\frac{dy}{dx}=f(ax+by+c)$

In this form the D.E. is not directly in Type-3 but by proper substitution it can be reduced to Type-3.

For this put $\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{by}+\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{t}$

⇒ $\text{a}+\text{b}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{dx}}$

Other form of this type

$\text{f}\left( \text{ax}+\text{by}+{{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)\text{dx}\pm \text{f}\left( \text{ax}+\text{by}+{{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)\text{dy}=0$

$\text{in this substitute ax}+\text{by}=\text{t}$

⇒ $\text{a}+\text{b}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{dx}}$
